title: HR Planning, Recruitment & Selection
simple_html:
Before you can manage people, you need the right people in the right jobs at the right time. That is the job of HR planning, recruitment and selection — the "getting people in" stage of HRM.

HR planning

Human resource planning forecasts how many people, with what skills, the organization will need, and compares that to who it already has. The gap tells you whether to hire, train, redeploy or reduce. It links the workforce to the business plan.

Recruitment

Recruitment is attracting a pool of suitable candidates. It starts with a job analysis (what the job involves), which produces a job description (the duties) and a person specification (the skills and qualities needed). You then choose where to advertise — internally, job boards, agencies, social media — to reach the right people.

Selection

Selection is choosing the best candidate from the pool. Common tools include CV screening, interviews, ability and personality tests, work-sample tasks and assessment centres. Good selection is reliable (consistent) and valid (actually predicts job success) and treats candidates fairly and legally.

Key takeaways

  • Plan the numbers and skills you need versus what you have.
  • Recruit by analysing the job, then attracting a strong candidate pool.
  • Select using valid, reliable, fair methods — structured interviews and work samples predict best.
  • A poor hire is expensive; investing in selection pays off.
  • A realistic preview of the job reduces early quitting.
intermediate_html:
Resourcing — HR planning, recruitment and selection — is how organizations secure the human capital their strategy requires. This lesson covers workforce planning, the job-analysis foundation, recruitment channels and employer branding, and the science of selection (reliability, validity and predictive methods), with a worked example.

1. Human resource planning (HRP)

HRP forecasts future demand for and supply of labour and plans to close the gap. Demand is driven by the business plan, productivity, and growth; techniques include managerial judgement, ratio-trend analysis and work-study. Internal supply is estimated from current headcount adjusted for turnover, promotions and retirements (e.g., skills inventories, Markov/transition analysis). External supply reflects the labour market. The demand–supply comparison yields actions: recruit, train/develop, redeploy, outsource, or downsize. Hard HRP focuses on numbers; soft HRP also plans culture, skills and behaviours.

2. Job analysis: the foundation

Recruitment begins with job analysis — systematically determining a role's tasks, responsibilities and the human attributes needed. It produces two documents: the job description (purpose, duties, reporting lines) and the person specification (essential and desirable knowledge, skills, abilities and other characteristics — KSAOs). These define the selection criteria, so errors here cascade.

3. Recruitment: attracting the pool

Recruitment aims to attract enough suitable applicants cost-effectively. Choices include internal vs external sourcing (internal builds morale and is cheaper/lower-risk; external brings fresh skills) and channels: referrals, job boards, agencies, professional networks and social media. Employer branding — the organization's reputation as a place to work — shapes who applies. A realistic job preview (RJP) — honest information about the role's pros and cons — lowers later turnover by aligning expectations, even though it may shrink the applicant pool.

4. Selection: choosing well

Selection methods are judged on two psychometric criteria. Reliability is consistency (same result on repetition / across assessors). Validity — especially predictive validity — is the degree to which a method forecasts actual job performance. The evidence (Schmidt & Hunter, 1998) ranks methods: work-sample tests, structured interviews and cognitive ability (GMA) tests predict best; unstructured interviews, references and graphology predict poorly. Structured interviews (same questions, behavioural/situational, scored against anchors) far outperform unstructured chats. Assessment centres combine multiple exercises and assessors for senior or high-volume roles.

5. Worked example

A firm hiring a sales manager defines the person spec (negotiation, resilience, data literacy). It uses a structured interview with situational questions, a role-play work sample (a simulated client negotiation) and a cognitive test. Two finalists interview equally well; the work sample reveals one freezes under pricing pressure. Because the work sample has high predictive validity, the firm hires the other — a decision an unstructured interview would have missed. Adding a realistic job preview (heavy travel, tough targets) leads one weaker-fit candidate to self-select out, saving an early resignation.

6. How to apply it

  • Start from HRP: know the quantity and capability you need before advertising.
  • Anchor everything in a sound job analysis; criteria must be job-relevant and lawful (avoid discrimination).
  • Prefer high-validity methods — structured interviews + work samples + ability tests.
  • Use employer branding and RJPs to attract and retain the right fit.
  • Measure cost-per-hire, time-to-fill, quality-of-hire and early turnover to improve the funnel.

This stage builds the Ability lever of AMO (Lesson 2) and feeds directly into Performance Management and Learning & Development.
full_html:
Resourcing — human resource planning (HRP), recruitment and selection — is the set of processes by which organizations acquire the human capital their strategy demands. Because the people brought in determine the ceiling of every downstream HR process, the evidentiary and legal stakes are high. This lesson develops the frameworks, the psychometric science of selection with seminal evidence, the role of employer branding and realistic previews, fairness/legal issues, and a worked validity calculation.

1. Human resource planning

HRP is the systematic process of forecasting an organization's future demand for and supply of labour and reconciling the two (Armstrong & Taylor, 2020). Demand forecasting derives required headcount and capabilities from the strategic plan using managerial judgement, ratio-trend analysis, regression on business drivers, and work-study. Internal supply forecasting projects the current workforce forward using wastage (turnover) rates, the stability index, promotion patterns, and stochastic models such as Markov/transition matrices and skills inventories. External supply reflects labour-market conditions, demographics and skills availability. The reconciliation produces a planned set of interventions — recruitment, development, redeployment, retention, outsourcing or restructuring. The distinction between hard HRP (quantitative headcount) and soft HRP (capabilities, culture, behaviours and engagement) mirrors the hard/soft divide of Lesson 1.

2. Job analysis and competency frameworks

The foundation of valid resourcing is job analysis — the systematic study of a role's content and context to identify tasks and the requisite knowledge, skills, abilities and other characteristics (KSAOs). Methods range from interviews, observation and diaries to structured instruments such as the Position Analysis Questionnaire and the O*NET framework. Outputs are the job description and person specification (classic schemes include Rodger's Seven-Point Plan). Increasingly, organizations use competency frameworks — clusters of behaviours linked to superior performance — to integrate selection, appraisal, development and reward around a common language (Boyatzis, 1982).

3. Recruitment, employer branding and realistic previews

Recruitment is the activity of generating a pool of qualified applicants. Strategic choices include the make-or-buy decision (develop internally vs hire externally) and channel mix (referrals, job boards, agencies, executive search, professional networks, social recruiting). The economics are tracked through yield ratios, cost-per-hire and time-to-fill. Employer branding — the package of functional, economic and psychological benefits associated with employment, and the identifying of the firm with these (Ambler & Barrow, 1996) — shapes both the size and the quality of the applicant pool and is grounded in signalling theory: candidates infer unobservable job attributes from observable signals. The realistic job preview (Wanous, 1992) provides balanced positive and negative information; meta-analytic evidence shows RJPs modestly reduce turnover and improve person–job fit and met expectations, partly through self-selection and a "vaccination" effect, at the cost of a smaller initial pool. Person–organization and person–job fit (Kristof, 1996) are the conceptual targets.

4. The science of selection

Selection decisions are predictions, and prediction quality is assessed psychometrically. Reliability is the consistency of measurement (test–retest, inter-rater, internal consistency). Validity is whether the method measures what it should and, critically, criterion/predictive validity — the correlation between a selection score and subsequent job performance. The seminal meta-analysis by Schmidt & Hunter (1998), synthesizing 85 years of data, established a validity ordering: general mental ability (GMA) tests (r ≈ 0.51), work-sample tests (≈ 0.54), structured interviews (≈ 0.51) are the strongest single predictors; conscientiousness, integrity tests and assessment centres add incremental validity; unstructured interviews (≈ 0.38), references (≈ 0.26), years of experience, age and graphology are weak. Combining GMA with a work sample or structured interview yields the highest validity. Structured interviews (Campion, Palmer & Campion, 1997) use job-analysis-based, situational or behavioural questions asked consistently and scored against behavioural anchors, dramatically outperforming unstructured interviews and reducing bias. Assessment centres deploy multiple exercises (in-tray, group, presentation, role-play) and multiple trained assessors.

5. Worked example: utility of valid selection

Validity is not academic — it has dollar value via utility analysis (Schmidt, Hunter et al.). Suppose a firm hires for a role where the standard deviation of performance in monetary terms (SDy) is $40,000 per year. Switching from an unstructured interview (validity r ≈ 0.20) to a structured interview plus work sample (combined r ≈ 0.60) raises validity by Δr = 0.40. With an average selection ratio and a standardized score of top applicants of, say, 1.0, the per-hire annual gain is approximately Δr × SDy × (standardized applicant score) = 0.40 × $40,000 × 1.0 = $16,000 per hire per year, multiplied by tenure and number of hires. This quantifies why investment in valid methods repays itself many times over and reframes selection as an economic decision, not a formality.

6. Fairness, bias and the law

Selection must be job-related and non-discriminatory. Adverse (disparate) impact occurs when a neutral practice disproportionately screens out a protected group (a common heuristic is the four-fifths rule). Some valid predictors (e.g., GMA) can produce subgroup differences, creating a validity–diversity tension that practitioners manage through work samples, structured behavioural assessment and removing irrelevant criteria. Structured methods also reduce similarity-attraction, halo and confirmation biases. This connects to Diversity & Inclusion concerns and to legal frameworks on equal opportunity.

7. Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Over-trusting the unstructured interview — the most popular yet among the least valid methods; "gut feel" is largely bias.
  • Skipping job analysis — without it, criteria are arbitrary, validity unknowable and legal defensibility weak.
  • Maximizing pool size over quality — bigger is not better; the goal is qualified applicants and good fit.
  • Ignoring the candidate experience — poor process damages employer brand and deters strong applicants.
  • Treating selection as one-way — candidates select too; RJPs and fair process matter for acceptance and retention.

8. Cross-links

Resourcing operationalizes the Ability lever of the AMO model (Lesson 2) and is shaped by the strategic philosophy of Lessons 1–2. It hands off to Performance Management (Lesson 4), which assesses the people brought in; to Learning & Development (Lesson 5), which closes capability gaps that recruitment cannot; and to Talent, Wellbeing & HR Analytics (Lesson 8), where quality-of-hire and selection utility are measured. Mastery means designing a defensible, high-validity, fair resourcing funnel that is explicitly tied to the workforce plan and employer brand.
